summary: Dan uses Amazon's reviews team as a cautionary example of how solving a problem can accidentally erase the problem itself. Amazon's original problem was customer confidence in buying without touching a product; the solution was reviews, but the team was literally named 'the reviews team,' so every hire and goal became 'make reviews go up and to the right' rather than 'increase customer confidence.' That narrowed focus meant Amazon almost missed other solutions to the same underlying problem, like letting customers ask other buyers questions or see fit information, and only unlocked those once someone forced the team to revisit the original problem instead of the solution. The takeaway he applies at Convoy is that naming a team or workstream after its current solution silently locks in tunnel vision, so it's worth periodically forcing a return to the original problem statement.
quote: But then they called the team reviews. And then everyone they ever hired from there on out was hired to the reviews team. And so every goal they set was how do we make reviews go up and to the right?... And so if you forget about the problem you're originally solving and you focus on improving the solution, you may actually miss like five other amazing opportunities.

summary: Building on the Amazon example, Dan says he deliberately tries to install a specific pushback habit into Convoy's culture: he wants employees to feel free to interrupt him mid-solution and ask what the original problem was and whether the current solution is actually still solving it. He frames this as something he wants directed at himself personally when he's 'going down on some solution,' not just a general principle, and admits it's hard to actually build into a company's culture rather than just state as a value. The actionable piece is narrow and concrete: it's a specific question ('what was the problem you started with? Are you sure this solves it?') meant to be asked routinely, not a vague call for more first-principles thinking.
quote: we need to remember it's important for people in the company to push back and say, I think we're too obsessed with this solution right now... And if you, like I want people to ask me that. If I'm like going down on some solution, like, damn, what was the problem? Are you sure this solved the problem?

summary: Convoy assumed truck drivers could be handled like Uber drivers and simply given a company phone if they didn't already have the right OS, but the model failed because trucking isn't a real-time job the way ride-hailing is. About 80-85% of drivers already had Android phones, so Convoy built Android-only at first and planned to hand out Android devices to the roughly 15-20% with iPhones. The flaw was behavioral, not technical: an Uber driver is glued to their phone constantly between jobs, while a trucker gets a notification about tomorrow's job while still doing today's, so they default to their own phone for real-time calls with dispatchers and only check the Convoy device at the end of the day. Realizing this too late meant engineering effort was misallocated before they pivoted quickly to build an iOS app.
quote: we assumed we could give drivers phones... it was maybe 80-ish, 85% of truck drivers had smartphones... an Uber driver is always focused on their phone 100% of the time... Whereas a trucker is doing a job right now, but getting a notification for a job that starts tomorrow from convoy... they're not going to look at the other phone until the end of the day, because it's not real-time

summary: Convoy's MVP built a real product on only one side of each two-sided relationship instead of building everything: an app for truck drivers/carriers but a manual back end for shippers, and a web interface for shippers but manual handling for carriers on the web side. In hindsight, Dan says he might have gone even further and picked just one single thing to build with technology (e.g., only the carrier experience) and done the rest by hand, because it's fine to run a business manually up front to validate the scenario before committing engineering and management bandwidth. His generalizable advice: identify the two most important steps in a multi-step transaction, build only those really well first, and do the rest manually so you don't fragment limited engineering and management bandwidth across too many problems at once.
quote: We didn't go build a web experience for trucking companies, and we didn't build an app for shippers... it's completely okay to use manual solutions to a problem up front to make sure you've nailed the scenarios, and to not fragment your management bandwidth and your engineering bandwidth across too many problems at once. So pick... the two, maybe, that are the most important, and build those really well up front... and do the other ones manually

summary: When raising Convoy's angel/seed round, Dan says investors are evaluating three specific things, not just the idea: first, whether the founder is fully committed and already working on it (not still employed elsewhere weighing whether to jump), because a founder who isn't already doing it is a much harder sell; second, whether the opportunity itself is credible, which is where storytelling matters most since most investors aren't domain experts; and third, and hardest, whether the founder can actually execute, i.e., can they assemble a team, or will the money just sit there with no one able to build it. He frames the third point as the one founders most often fail to de-risk, since it's easy to quit a job and tell a compelling story but much harder to prove you can already build the team.
quote: there are three things that really matter. One is the person you're speaking with has to believe that you are passionate about it you're committed to it... Two is the opportunity obviously matters... What's hard often is can you go do it? Do you have four engineers or two engineers committed and working on this right now with you or if I give you the money is there now risk you're never going to be able to go hire the team to deliver on this?

summary: To de-risk the 'can you actually execute' concern before raising money, Dan recruited five or six engineers to work on Convoy part-time, unpaid, before the seed round closed, rather than waiting until funding to build a team. He says this was more decisive for investors than anything else, more so than the deck or the story, because it directly proved execution risk was already being solved: the team's names, pictures, and willingness to serve as references were on the pitch materials. The lesson is that founders should treat assembling a partial, working team as part of the fundraising deliverable itself, not something to defer until after money is raised.
quote: we had five or six people working on this who were engineers and helping us when we wanted to go raise money that was the thing that made all the difference the other stuff I mentioned is super important having the engineering team doing it and committed and like their names on a slide and their pictures and like them willing to be references changed it

summary: Dan argues founders should classify their business as 'linear' versus 'binary/scale-or-die' when pitching investors, and that this framing matters more than the sector's popularity. Binary businesses like gaming, messaging, or certain social networks only work if they hit massive scale or go viral — there's no reliable middle outcome, and mid-sized versions of these businesses (he cites Path) essentially fail. Linear businesses like trucking/logistics instead convert effort and homework into proportional results, so Dan explicitly pitched Convoy to investors as a lower-risk, effort-driven bet rather than a lottery-ticket bet requiring a viral spark, which he believes made it feel safer to investors who didn't know the industry.
quote: is this a linear business or a nonlinear business is this a binary business or is this a business that has sort of a gradient... there are a lot of businesses that really aren't going to be successful unless they're massive... whereas there are other businesses that are much more linear and effort and I think they're less risky... trucking for example and logistics I felt like it was less risky because I felt like we could put the energy in and do the homework
